content.problemStatement:
Andy got a box of candies for Christmas. In fact, he discovered that the box contained several identical smaller boxes, and they could contain even smaller boxes, and so on. Formally, we say that candies are boxes of level 0, and for 1≤i≤n1 \leq i \leq n1≤i≤n, a level iii box contains aia_iai​ boxes of level i−1i-1i−1. The largest box has level nnn. Andy realized that it can take quite a long time to open all the boxes before he actually gets to eat some candies, so he put the box aside in frustration.

But today being his birthday, some friends came to visit Andy, and Andy decided to share some candies with them. In order to do that, he must open some of the boxes. Naturally, Andy can not open a box that is still inside an unopened box. If Andy wants to retrieve XXX candies, what is the least number of boxes he must open? You must help him answer many such queries. Each query is independent.


Input Format

The first line contains two integers nnn and mmm, which refer to the level of the largest box, and the number of queries respectively.
The second line contains nnn integers a1,…,ana_1, \ldots, a_na1​,…,an​.
The third line contains mmm integers X1,…,XmX_1, \ldots, X_mX1​,…,Xm​.


Output Format

Print mmm integers each in a new line, ithi^{th}ith of them equal to the smallest number of boxes Andy must open in order to retrieve at least XiX_iXi​ candies.
constraints: It is guaranteed that the total number of candies is at least XiX_iXi​ for all iii.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Testcase 1: The only candy is contained in five levels of boxes.

Explanation:
Testcase 2: In the third query, for 13 candies, Andy should open the largest box, two level-2 boxes, and finally five of six available level-1 boxes. Each of those boxes will contain 3 level-0 boxes (which are candies). So he'll have 15 candies in total, but he needs only 13 of them.
